description: Single conversation produces three memories: a decision, an architecture choice, and a convention

turns.text: We need a proper background-job system. Right now we shell out to cron and write our own retry logic per script. Tell me what we should pick and how to structure it.

The mature options in the Node ecosystem are BullMQ (Redis-backed), Hatchet (Postgres-backed, durable workflows), and Inngest (managed). Given you already run Redis and want zero new infra, BullMQ is the easiest first jump. For the structure: one queue per job *family* (not per job), keep the worker process distinct from the web process, and route slow jobs to a dedicated queue with its own concurrency limit so they can't starve the fast ones.

Okay. Decisions: (1) we'll adopt BullMQ as the standard background-job system across all services. No more ad-hoc cron + scripts. (2) The architecture is a separate `worker` process per service, sharing the same Redis instance as the web process but running on a different deployment with its own scaling rules. (3) Convention: every job handler must be idempotent — same input applied twice produces the same end state. We rely on idempotency for at-least-once retries.

Three solid calls. On (3): make the idempotency contract explicit by giving every job a `dedupe_key` and writing a small `processed_jobs` table that the handler checks first. That turns 'must be idempotent' from a guideline into something the runtime enforces. Also worth adding to the runbook: jobs that touch external APIs need request-deduplication on the API side too, because BullMQ retries can trigger duplicate side-effects even with an idempotent handler.

expected.note: Pick BullMQ as the standard background-job system.
Separate worker process per service, distinct from web process.
Every job handler must be idempotent. 'idempoten' is a stem matching idempotent/idempotency.
